title: The childhood cancer survival gap: 80% vs 20% and a clinical trial desert in Africa
primary_estimand: Condition Colonialism Index (CCI) for childhood cancer trials in Africa
certainty: high (registry data, complete enumeration within ClinicalTrials.gov)
body: How does clinical trial activity for childhood cancer in Africa compare with the disease burden? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov for interventional studies of childhood cancer, pediatric oncology, childhood leukemia, Wilms tumor, neuroblastoma, and retinoblastoma across Africa, the United States, India, and Brazil, and separately queried Burkitt lymphoma and Kaposi sarcoma. Africa returned approximately three childhood cancer trials versus one thousand six hundred fifty-seven in the United States, yielding a Condition Colonialism Index of roughly five hundred fifty-three. Five-year childhood cancer survival is eighty percent in high-income countries but only twenty percent in Sub-Saharan Africa, a sixty-point gap representing the largest survival disparity in oncology. Burkitt lymphoma, the commonest childhood cancer in equatorial Africa and curable with modern chemotherapy, has near-zero African trial activity. Wilms tumor and retinoblastoma both exceed ninety percent survival in high-income settings yet have no registered African trials. This analysis is limited to one public registry and cannot capture national platforms or twinning protocols.
ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used in early drafting. The final text was rewritten by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.

checks.value: Africa ~3 trials vs US 1,657; 80% vs 20% survival gap
Wilms tumor and retinoblastoma: >90% curable in HICs, near-zero African trials
US, India, Brazil queried as comparators
single registry, cannot capture national/WHO/twinning protocols
